data.title: CrowdStrike: Premium Valuation, But Scalable AI-Native Growth Justifies The Bet
data.description:
The combination of a growing digital world and cyber threats asks for a scalable solution, which CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:CRWD) offers with its Falcon platform making the company a pureplay cloud cybersecurity play. Cybersecurity is playing an increasingly important role as the world is becoming more and more digital. With more tasks being offloaded to artificial intelligence, more cloud adoption and data-driven decision making, the need for scalable security solutions is paramount. The current global tensions around the world also make it more important for enterprises and governments to bolster security against cyber warfare, which has become another axis of warfare.

The company recently announced that it would be laying off 5% of its staff, a move that could improve margins. The company cited that AI is flattening the hiring curve. While I do believe there is a major difference between hiring and firing, the adoption of AI could make CrowdStrike’s business and products even more scalable.

In this report, I discuss why I believe the company is current XX YY.

Total Addressable Market For AI-Native Security Set To Grow To $250 Billion

That there is huge demand for AI-native security platforms becomes rather clear when we look at the $116 billion total addressable market. With its Falcon platform, the company can capitalize on demand. For reference purposes, for FY26 (CrowdStrike’s financial year runs from February 2025 through January 2026) the company expects $4.8 billion in sales. So, there is a major addressable market relative to the existing markets and against expected sales. Moreover, by 2029 the market is expected to have more than doubled to $250 billion. So, CrowdStrike can grow its sales easily to $10 billion through maintaining market share, but I believe the company could expand its market share. An obvious risk is when CrowdStrike’s own systems fail causing widespread disruption as we saw in 2024 when CrowdStrike distributed a fault update to its Falcon Sensor platform causing widespread IT outages.

CrowdStrike Exceeds $1 Billion in Quarterly Subscription Revenues

The most recent annual results show 29% growth in revenues to $3.95 billion with subscription revenue growth of 31% and net new annual revenue revenues being $807 million. The company ended the year with $4.24 billion in annual recurring revenues with 80% gross margins on subscriptions and 21% core operating margins and 27% free cash flow margin delivering a free cash flow $1.07 billion.

CrowdStrike exceeded estimates marginally by 0.6% on revenues and a stronger 4.5% beat on earnings per share.

CrowdStrike Eyes Up To 22% Growth In FY26

For the first quarter of FY26, the CrowdStrike is expecting around $1.1 billion in sales which is not surprising given the annual recurring revenue of $4.24 billion that the company ended with in FY25. The operating profit points at margins of 15.7% to 16.3%, which I believe reflects seasonal spending patterns and investments in AI capabilities and infrastructure. For the full year, the company expects $4.47 billion to $4.8 billion in sales pointing at sales growth of 20% to 21.5% with margins of 18% to 20.5%.

What Is CrowdStrike Stock Worth?

In some way, the growth rates are impressive and we see big addressable market. So, demand does not seem to be an issue. So, we do not really need to overanalyze the results. What I am more interested in is whether the stock provides upside. Quite often we see that high growth companies have a lot of growth already priced in. That makes running a comprehensive analysis on stock price targets important. I am using two methods to value the stock, the first one is an EV/EBITDA method and the second one is the EV/Sales method.

To determine multi-year price targets using the EV/EBITDA method, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Valuing CrowdStrike according to the EV/EBITDA method is challenging. The company is overvalued against peers and its median EV/EBITDA multiple is negative. To come up with a target, I am using the forward multiple using the average EBITDA over the next three years and I am applying a 20% discount to that which gives a $461.53 price target and 8% upside. This is based on 27% annual growth in EBITDA and 23.6% growth in free cash flow.

Using an EV/sales valuation, we would get to a price target of $490.64 implying 15% upside. Given that the company’s valuation is rather steep, if we take out the net cash position and value the stock accordingly we get to a $471.35 price target implying 10% upside.

With both valuations in mind, it is rather clear that the stock is expensive. What I do like is the growth trajectory on EBITDA and free cash flow and the net cash position. So, for now I am rating the stock a Buy mostly based on the upside seen even if we discount the trading multiple.

Conclusion: CrowdStrike Is For Investor Who Believes In The Business

CrowdStrike is expensive at its near-term expected earnings and sales. However, this is a stock that could make you regret buying in 10-years from now. That means that the buy rating is not necessarily based on strong fundamental valuations currently but is more a buy opportunity for the future on increasing demand for the company’s product. So, this could have a place in a speculative portfolio.

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: $60 Oil Can't Last
data.description:
Previous thesis and new developments

I last covered Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (NYSE:EPD) in mid-March 2025. That article was entitled "Why I Like Enterprise Products Partners Better Than Williams Companies”. It argued a strong buy rating with an emphasis on the role of natural gas for the company’s earnings. Quote:

I expect a boost in domestic natural gas production and more favorable regulations ahead. This view has led me to feel bullish toward stocks with a large exposure to natural gas such as EPD and WMB. WMB is a solid investment. Its higher valuation ratios can be justified to a large extent by its better growth and profitability metrics. However, I see an even strong return/risk profile in EPD due to its lower valuation even when adjusted for growth and dividends, better consistency, and higher current yield.

Since then, the macroeconomic landscape has shifted materially. The tariff disputes that started on April 2, 2025 (aka the "Liberation Day") have heightened the risks of a slowdown of the U.S. economy and perhaps even the global economy. A direct consequence of such uncertainties is the sharp decline of oil price, as you can see from the following chart. The spot price of Brent crude oil dropped by ~17% since my last analysis and currently hovers around $60 only as of this writing. EPS shares prices were pressured in tandem as seen. In the subsequent sections of this article, I will explain A) why the current oil prices won’t last, and B) why EPD share prices are well-positioned to benefit if/when oil prices recovery occurs.

Why $60 oil price can’t last

I see several catalysts that could support an oil price rebound in the near term. To keep the discussion focus, here I will limit myself to the top two catalysts on my list: the refilling of the U.S. strategic petroleum reserve ("SPR") and also the relatively low current oil inventory.

For the first catalyst, the next chart taken from EIA data provides a broader historical context by displaying the U.S. SPR on a weekly basis since the 1980s. The latest data point (as of the week of May 2, 2025), the U.S. SPR stocks stand at 399,122 thousand barrels. This is not only a far cry from the peak of ~750,000 thousand barrels observed around ~2010 but also among the lowest levels since the 1980s. During the COVID pandemic, the U.S. deployed a large portion of the SPR to counter fuel price surges and high inflation.

In the past 1~2 years, the U.S. has started to gradually refill the SPR reserved. However, the current SPR reading is still far from adequate by historical standard and I expect the refilling to keep going. I further expect this additional demand to help catalyze oil price recovery.

As for the second catalyst, the level of current US Crude Oil Stocks is also near a multi-year low as you can see from the following chart source from YChart. As of the lasted reading taken on May 2, 2025, the current inventory is 837.5 million barrels. The inventory has declined substantially from almost 1.2 billion barrels five years ago, and the current reading is among the lowest levels in this period. Such a low inventory is another important factor to support higher oil prices in my model.

Earnings and crude oil prices

My view is that EPD is well-poised to benefit if/when oil price recovery occurs. Historically, the correlation between EPD’s operating cash flow has been positive with respect to Brend crude price as you can see from the following chart. Over the long term (say the past 20 years), the average correlation has been 0.345, a quite positive level. The correlation in the past 1~2 years has been hovering around 0.88, significantly above the historical average.

Besides my expectation for higher oil prices, I am also seeing a few other factors that could help the partnership to continue growing its earnings. One of the Trump Administration’s main platforms is to increase the amount of domestic energy produced. As such, I expect to see more laxed regulations that can help fast track drilling activity, creating high volume for most of Enterprise’s business. Furthermore, I also expect the demand from Europe to rise strongly given the ongoing geopolitical conflicts in that region, especially for EPD’s LNG business.

Other risks and final thoughts

As a reflection of such growth potential, the next chart shows consensus EPS estimate for EPD stock in the next 6 years. As seen, its EPS is projected to grow from $2.80 in FY 2025 to $3.41 in FY 2030. This represents an estimated compound annual growth rate ("CAGR") of 4.0% over this period.

As a downside risk, my estimate for its growth rate shows a larger variance that the above consensus estimates. My approach for estimating long-term organic growth rate is detailed in my earlier articles. Here, I will only present the end results. The method involves the use of its return on capital employed (“ROCE”) and also growth RR (reinvestment rate). The ROCE for EPD has been quite consistent in recent years (see the next chart below) and my projection for 2025 is 15.2%.

The growth RR is a large source of uncertainty here. As announced in its recent earnings report (ER), released at the end of April 2025, EPD has many construction programs ongoing. The ER also provided the following guidance for the growth capital expenditures range in the next few years: $4.0B to $4.5B in 2025; $2.0B to $2.5B in 2026 as you can see from the following ER slide. Such a large variation of growth CAPEX translates into a large variance in its RR raging from about 21% to over 40%. Assuming a ROCE of 15.2%, the organic growth rate would be in the range of 3.2% (21% RR * 15.2% ROCE = 3.2% growth rate) and 6.1% (40% RR * 15.2% ROCE = 6.1% growth rate).

Although, even with a conservative estimate of its RR and growth rate of 3.2%, the stock already provides a highly skewed return/risk ratio at its current valuation already. The stock is currently priced at 10.95X FWD P/E ratio and provides a dividend yield of 6.98%. Thus, its PEGY ratio (the P/E ratio divided by the sum of the earnings growth rate and dividend yield) is only 1.07 even with only 3.2% growth rate, very close to the ideal threshold of 1x most dividend-growth investors consider as the gold standard.

To close, this follow-up analysis on EPD is triggered by the large change in oil prices and also the growth plan provided in its EPD’s recent ER. I see several factors that can catalyze an oil price recovery and EPS growth for EPD. Combined with EPD’s current P/E multiples, I see a very skewed return/risk profile and thus maintain my strong buy rating.

data.title: What The Market Misunderstands About Sally Beauty -- And Why That May Soon Change
data.description:
In the realm of investors looking for undervalued businesses, Sally Beauty Holdings (NYSE: NYSE:SBH) presents a compelling case to us because of the core business and the apparent mismatch in how the market currently sees the risks at play at Sally Beauty. Upon first glance, Sally Beauty may seem like just another struggling retail company, but that basic view overlooks a deeper commitment by management to repurchasing shares, reducing debt and the fact that it may be entering an entirely new phase focused on shareholder returns.

In the past few years, Sally Beauty has been quite aggressive at deleveraging and funnelling free cash flow into reducing debt and buying back shares. In fact, since 2019, it has used almost 100% of its current market capitalization to either pay down debt or buy back shares. This kind of capital allocation already commands attention, yet the market seems to be fairly indifferent or is at least far more fearful, with the share price down over 40% from recent highs over the past year.

Business Fundamentals and Disciplined Capital Allocation

Looking back almost 20 years to 2006, when Sally Beauty was spun out of Alberto-Culver, it was a highly leveraged business with debt at around six times earnings. Since then, especially in the last five years, management has worked hard to reduce that leverage. As of Q1 FY2025, the company maintains a level of debt that is just under twice its annual net earnings, with a further goal to reduce this leverage to between 1.5 and 1.7 times annual earnings. By achieving this target, which is expected within the next couple of quarters, Sally Beauty would be far more financially flexible and would be able to focus more aggressively on shareholder returns through stock repurchases or otherwise investing in the business.

This transition could be a turning point for the business. With far fewer constraints on the balance sheet, it's possible that Sally Beauty could direct more of its cash generation toward enhancing shareholder value. This already seems to be the management's intent. Since 2022, there have already been $210 million worth of share repurchases and around $455 million put toward debt reduction. This is a total of $665 million, which is about 70% of the company's current market cap. Even if we were to assume no growth, simply maintaining this level of buybacks would shrink the float quite substantially, and provide per-share earnings and cash flow growth through this type of financial maneuvering alone.

Sally Beauty's business model, which is largely driven by recurring purchases of hair colour and hair care products, provides a relatively stable cash generation mechanism. If we look at the fact that over 70% of sales are from hair products alone, and about a third of sales come exclusively from private label brands, which not only carry higher margins but also tend to reinforce customer loyalty and pricing power, Sally Beauty is not just another retailer. These private label products give Sally Beauty more control over its supply chain costs and branding, and also insulate the company from direct price competition with larger national labels. Sally Beauty is able to maintain attractive gross margins this way, while developing unique value propositions for customers, especially those requiring consistent quality and value. The stickiness of these products, which are often tied to ongoing hair colour systems or regimens, increases repeat purchases and keeps revenues stable even during economic fluctuations.

We can also turn to very healthy gross margins hovering at around 49% in recent quarters, which reflect not only effective pricing but also the impact of the already mentioned private label brands. With more control over costs and branding, the business is able to command better margins than national labels. Even compared to other competitors in the beauty retail space, the gross profit margin is quite solid. Looking at Ulta Beauty, for example, a much larger peer, they maintain a gross profit margin around 38%, which tells us that Sally's more focused product mix and an emphasis on private label products is indeed a competitive edge. The strength of this profit margin reinforces Sally's ability to deal with pricing pressure while maintaining profitability in a competitive landscape. With Return On Invested Capital (ROIC), which comfortably exceeds a Weighted Average Cost of Capital (OTC:WACC), the business is clearly deploying shareholder funds efficiently. Further, the cash-to-debt ratio supports the argument that Sally has the flexibility to endure competitive pressure while continuing to prioritize capital returns.

Market Mispricing & Comparison

The valuation backdrop is what makes this the most compelling. Sally Beauty is currently trading at under 5x forward earnings, which is far below historical averages and quite a bit lower than peers like Ulta Beauty, which is typically in the range of 15x to 20x forward earnings. This gap in valuation is quite striking when considering Sally's consistent free cash flow and capital returns, which we have discussed. While Ulta Beauty commands a premium due to broader brand presence and higher revenue growth, Sally's solid profitability per dollar of revenue is being almost completely ignored by the market.

This thesis of shareholder returns becomes even more interesting when looking at other capital allocation stories. Companies such as those in the technology sector will receive praise for stock buybacks at much more elevated valuations, while Sally Beauty is doing quite the opposite, repurchasing shares in a responsible way at a significant discount to intrinsic valuations such as free cash flow and other multiples. In just the past two years, Sally Beauty has retired approximately 10% of outstanding shares, and there's no reason to believe that if current free cash flow levels continue, this pace couldn't accelerate. During the most recent quarter, Sally Beauty still had $520.8 million worth of remaining authorization for its share repurchase program, which is more than half of its entire market capitalization. This should tell us that buybacks are not just an afterthought or a recent trend, but a major focus of management's capital allocation strategy to shareholders. Ironically, this story should be straightforward. With a strong free cash flow, reducing debt, and accelerating buybacks, the current valuation screams of a business in distress. The market is treating Sally Beauty as if it is in secular decline, which, from everything I have seen, could be a mistake. While growth is only modest, we see the cost structure improving due to the "Fuel for Growth" initiative announced by Sally's leadership team, which is expected to drive $120 million in annual run rate savings by 2026. These are expected to come from a reduction in SG&A expenses, streamlining supply chain operations, and increasing efficiency across store operations. Management has also indicated that a large portion of early savings will come from renegotiated supplier contracts and improving labor scheduling. The long-term plan includes reinvesting a portion of savings toward digital infrastructure and improving the customer experience while bolstering share repurchases or, potentially, initiating a dividend. The first wave of savings, which could be about $70 million, is expected to be realized by the end of fiscal 2025. All of this should enhance margins and provide a boost to cash flow available for capital returns.

Risks, Reality, and Final Thoughts

Let's not forget, risks remain. Sally Beauty still tends to operate in a very competitive retail environment with significant pressures from direct consumer beauty brands, ecommerce channels, and salon supply chains. Even as we acknowledge these risks, however, the valuation appears to more than compensate for this. At the current price, the market seems to be pricing Sally Beauty as if free cash flow is eroding, not growing, and the capital returns are at risk. Both of these assumptions appear to be contradicted by management's solid track record and upcoming capital allocation potential.

This could be where the opportunity lies. Sally Beauty is not a fast-growing disruptor. As a mature, steady business with consistent cash flows that focuses on operating efficiency and strategic capital returns, the market's excitement tends to go elsewhere. It may not capture the spotlight like tech innovators and other hyper-growth retailers, but the shareholder focus is quietly building value as far as I can tell. For value investors, this sort of setup based on dependable earnings, disciplined capital allocation, and aggressive, responsible buybacks often represents one of the most durable long-term paths to outperform as an investment. Comparing this to other high-growth names that rarely return capital and trade on lofty multiples, Sally Beauty offers a compelling case as a potentially undervalued quality business. It is a free cash flow generator with a track record of shareholder-friendly actions, operating in a recurring revenue business at a valuation that reflects almost none of that reality.

With second-quarter earnings not factored into this writing, it remains to be seen if other risks present themselves. However, we may see this valuation gap close as investors begin to realize the long-term potential in Sally Beauty that may be hiding in plain sight. Further research is certainly warranted, but for those focused on cash flow, capital discipline, and long-term compounding, this is a name that deserves attention.

data.title: Davide Campari: Q1 2025 Fell Short Of My Expectations
data.description:
The results for Q1 2025 raised many doubts among investors in Davide Campari (OTCPK:DVDCF); tariffs will have a significant impact and, a few weeks ago, management decided to change the company's strategy:

The main reason I've decided to pause M&A for now is our net debt... we need to continue to get that down. "I wouldn't say never, but I'd say for the next couple of years, few years, (the priorities) I see are deleverage, focus on our core portfolio and really optimize the brands that we have already acquired.

CEO Simon Hunt

Acquisitions will be put on hold and the focus will be on improving what is under management control. Tariffs are an exogenous factor, but cost optimization and a focus on core brands can limit the damage. In Q1 2025, this is exactly what management wants to emphasize: overcoming this difficult period with the resources available to the company.

Highlights Q1 2025

Compared to Q1 2024, net sales decreased by 4.20%, but this decline was exacerbated by two non-recurring factors: an unfavorable calendar and US logistics delays.

As regards the calendar, the company refers to the Easter holidays, which this year fell in April, whereas last year they were in March. Revenues from these holidays will be seen in the next quarter, so comparing March 2024 with March 2025 is rather misleading. Without the “Easter effect” and logistical delays, net sales would have fallen by 1.10%. This is not a good result, but it is definitely better than -4.20%.

Let's now take a look at the individual brands in the most important segment, House of Aperitifs.

They account for 44% of total sales and have declined by 1% compared to last year. Frankly, I expected something better, regardless of the fact that Q1 2024 benefited from the Easter holidays. Aperol did not grow, but at least consolidated last year's strong growth, +15% in the US and +25% in Germany. Campari fared worse, not only because of weaker demand in the US, but also because it failed to replicate last year's exceptional performance in Brazil: group sales rose by +77% in Q1 2024. Crodino performed better, as it did not suffer as much as the others from the 'Easter effect' in Europe.

This is the most important segment for the company, and it is where its competitive advantage lies. I would have expected at least some growth, but looking at the other segments, I realize that this is not even the most negative aspect of this quarterly report.

All other segments are suffering much more; in the case of Cognac & Champagne, the decline is in double digits. Given these figures, it is clear why the new CEO Simon Hunt wants to revolutionize this company: there are too many weak brands that should not even be in the portfolio. DVDCF's competitive advantage lies in its popularity during aperitifs, not in Cognac or Champagne. In recent years, the company has made a series of acquisitions in order to diversify its revenues from Aperol and Campari, but the result has perhaps not been as hoped.

Net debt has skyrocketed but has not been followed by growing net income. DVDCF has failed to create the right synergies between its various acquisitions and existing brands, which is why management is retracing its steps. Unprofitable brands will be sold and costs will be optimized.

This process could take years, and the goal is to save 200 basis points on revenues by 2027. Seventy percent of the process has already been completed, but the real results will begin to be visible in the second half of 2025.

However, looking at organic SG&A growth, we can already see a sharp decline this quarter compared to the past. I don't know if DVDCF will actually succeed in its intent, but I don't think cost savings alone will solve the situation. It is essential that revenues return to growth, but this quarterly report has not helped to resolve my doubts.

Tariffs are expected to weigh €25 million on EBIT in 2025, about 4% of EBIT in 2024, but this is not the only factor to consider. Twenty-nine percent of revenue comes from the US, and the depreciation of the dollar could be a significant headwind for 2025. Both are potentially short-term issues, but the latest results have also increased my concerns about long-term growth.

Campari's share price has fallen over the last 18 years, and given that its competitive advantage remained strong, I considered it a buy in my last few articles. However, Aperol and Campari are struggling to grow, and I'm not sure that the Easter effect is entirely to blame. I believe there is more to it, perhaps less interest in these brands, and this is what leads me to consider it a hold today. Furthermore, the sharp decline in all other segments certainly does not help, because it is clear that DVDCF cannot rely on them too much: the diversification process has not gone as planned. Yet we are not in a recession, and I wonder how these brands would perform if there were one.

Personally, I had a small position in DVDCF and after this quarterly report I cut it significantly, taking advantage of the fact that the stock was up several percentage points since I bought it. I wanted to keep a few shares in my portfolio because I believe there is some chance that Simon Hunt's restructuring plan could bring the company back to the top; after all, Aperol and Campari remain brands that are appreciated all over the world. The point is that I currently believe there are better opportunities than DVDCF in the long term, and I have invested part of the proceeds from the sale in Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Novo Nordisk (NVO).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
